elements.sent-2411-001: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a multi-contrast imaging technique in which different contrast images share similar structural information.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, conventional diffusion models struggle to effectively leverage this structural similarity.
elements.sent-2411-003: Recently, the Schrödinger Bridge (SB), a nonlinear extension of the diffusion model, has been proposed to establish diffusion paths between any distributions, allowing the incorporation of guided priors.
elements.sent-2411-004: This study proposes an SB-based, multi-contrast image-guided reconstruction framework that establishes a diffusion bridge between the guiding and target image distributions.
elements.sent-2411-005: By using the guiding image along with data consistency during sampling, the target image is reconstructed more accurately.
elements.sent-2411-006: To better address structural differences between images, we introduce an inversion strategy from the field of image editing, termed $\mathbf{I ^2$SB-inversion.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Experiments on a paried T1 and T2-FLAIR datasets demonstrate that $\mathbf{I ^2$SB-inversion achieve a high acceleration up to 14.4 and outperforms existing methods in terms of both reconstruction accuracy and stability.}
elements.sent-2411-008: However, long acquisition times greatly hinder its broader clinical applications.
elements.sent-2411-009: A widely adopted strategy to mitigate this limitation is undersampling measurements in k-space, and then reconstructing images from the undersampled data by leveraging prior information.
elements.sent-2411-010: In traditional methods like Compressed Sensing (CS)\cite{lustig2008compressed,haldar2010compressed , these priors are typically hand-crafted for specific applications, such as sparsity priors \cite{ehrhardt2014joint,zhao2012image,majumdar2015improving , low-rank priors \cite{shin2014calibrationless,lingala2011accelerated,jin2016general,lin2018efficient , and group sparsity/low-rank priors\cite{trzasko2008highly,yang2016sparse .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Constructing such priors is often non-trivial, and these methods usually achieve limited acceleration factors.
elements.sent-2411-012: With the rise of deep learning (DL) in recent years, DL-based MRI reconstruction methods have gained substantial attention and demonstrated promising results.
elements.sent-2411-013: A popular approach is using end-to-end learning, which maps undersampled k-space data or aliased images to high-quality, artifact-free images through supervised learning\cite{huang2023vigu, huang2022unsupervised, liang2020deep, han2019k, peng2022deepsense, oh2020unpaired, nakarmi2020multi, huang2019deep .}
elements.sent-2411-014: This approach leverages priors learned from historical data, removing the need for manual prior design and tedious parameter tuning in traditional reconstruction methods.
elements.sent-2411-015: A classic example of this approach is the "unrolling" method \cite{modl, sun2016deep,zhang2018ista, cui2021equilibrated , which unrolls the iterative reconstruction process into a deep neural network.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Within this framework, the model can learn hyperparameters or regularization terms\cite{ke2021learned,huang2021deep , and even data fidelity terms\cite{cheng2020learning directly from the data, leading to optimized, data-driven solutions.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: It therefore offers improved performance over traditional techniques.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, supervised learning approaches typically require large amounts of fully sampled data for training, which is challenging to acquire, and often exhibit limited generalization when the sampling pattern or data characteristics change.
elements.sent-2411-019: Another DL-based approach for MRI reconstruction is based on distribution learning, which characterizes underlying image distributions to generate samples with the guidance of acquired k-space data.
elements.sent-2411-020: Distribution learning is typically implemented using generative models that can be trained in an unsupervised manner, alleviating the need for fully sampled data.
elements.sent-2411-021: Unlike end-to-end approaches that rely on paired training data, these models are independent of fixed data mappings, improving generalization across diverse sampling patterns and datasets.
elements.sent-2411-022: A notable example of this approach is diffusion models\cite{song2019generative,ho2020denoising,song2020score , which have advanced significantly in recent years.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Diffusion models establish an invertible pipeline between a data distribution and a Gaussian distribution by learning the gradient of the log data distribution, known as the score function, allowing the sampling process to progressively denoise from Gaussian noise to generate high-quality images.
elements.sent-2411-024: There are mainly two strategies for applying diffusion models in MRI reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-025: The first strategy introduces new conditional generation techniques during sampling to enforce data consistency mapping.
elements.sent-2411-026: For instance, AdaDiff \cite{gungor2023adaptive and Score MRI \cite{chung2022score incorporate guiding gradients into the random sampling process, while DDS \cite{chung2022diffusion and MCG \cite{chung2022improving project samples onto a clean data space, resolve data consistency, and then return to the diffusion pathway.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The second strategy \cite{cui2023spirit,cui2023physics,xie2022measurement designs new stochastic differential equations (SDEs) for diffusion models to enforce the diffusion process more consistent with MRI physics.}
elements.sent-2411-028: While both strategies yield promising reconstruction results, they rely solely on acquired k-space data for sampling guidance.
elements.sent-2411-029: Given that MRI is a multi-contrast imaging modality, different contrast images share similar structural information\cite{yang2020model,ozbey2023unsupervised .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Accordingly, prior information from one contrast image can be leveraged to guide the reconstruction of others.
elements.sent-2411-031: However, these priors are rarely utilized in diffusion-based MR reconstructions, as current conditional diffusion models can not fully leverage them for guided MR reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-032: Some studies use joint distribution diffusion models to characterize correlations among multi-contrast images\cite{xie2024joint,meng2024multi,li2024dual,yang2024dual , yet this approach does not fully capture their structural correspondence.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Further exploration of guidance strategies is needed to better leverage multi-contrast priors for diffusion based reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-034: Recently, a new class of distribution learning methods, known as Schrödinger Bridge (SB) \cite{liu20232,kim2023unpaired , has been proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-035: SB is a nonlinear extension of diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-036: Unlike conventional diffusion models that primarily focus on Gaussian distributions, it provides a more generalized approach that can establish diffusion bridges between any two arbitrary distributions.
elements.sent-2411-037: An initial attempt of SB-based MR reconstruction is the Fourier-Constrained Diffusion Bridges (FDB) method \cite{mirza2023learning .}
elements.sent-2411-038: FDB directly maps the aliased images to their corresponding artifact-free images through SB path.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, this approach remains limited to signal-contrast image reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-040: In this study, we propose a completely different approach that using SB for multi-contrast image guided reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-041: Specifically, a diffusion bridge is established between the guiding and target image contrast distributions through the SB model.
elements.sent-2411-042: During sampling, the guiding image can be effectively transformed into a high-fidelity target image, achieving a high acceleration rate with improved reconstruction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-043: A well-known issue in guided reconstruction or image translation between multi-contrast or multi-modality images is their structural differences, particularly for fine details.
elements.sent-2411-044: These differences arise from the varying tissue characteristics that different imaging modalities rely on, as each modality is influenced by distinct aspects of tissue properties.
elements.sent-2411-045: Under the limitations of 'regression to the mean'\cite{kendall2017uncertainties , these differences are often overlooked, leading to a degradation in performance." To solve this issue, we introduced a concept in image editing field: inversion\cite{huberman2024edit .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: It refers to reversing the generation process to extract latent variables (typically noise maps in diffusion models), which enable the generation model to accurately reconstruct a given image.
elements.sent-2411-047: These latent variables can subsequently be incorporated with techniques such as text prompts to handle image editing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-048: In our proposed SB-based guided reconstruction, we introduce inversion to identify the true guiding variable along the SB path that corresponds to the image being reconstructed, correcting the reconstruction errors caused by subtle differences between multi-contrast images.
elements.sent-2411-049: We propose a novel guided MR reconstruction framework based on the Schrödinger Bridge, exploiting its ability to establish a diffusion bridge between the image distribution and the guiding image distribution.
elements.sent-2411-050: This approach achieves a high acceleration rate up to 14.4 and outperforms conventional diffusion-based reconstruction methods and supervised unrolling techniques.
elements.sent-2411-051: We introduce an inversion strategy to correct reconstruction errors arising from structural discrepancies between the guiding and target images, thereby enhancing reconstruction performance.
elements.sent-2411-052: The following sections of the paper are organized as follows: Section \ref{Background introduces the background, Section \ref{theory and methods describes the proposed method, and Section \ref{experiments provides the experimental results.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Discussion and conclusion are given in Section \ref{discussion and Section \ref{conclusions .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: SGM is a general framework for diffusion models that describes the diffusion process as the solution of stochastic differential equations (SDEs).
elements.sent-2411-055: Given an initial state \( \mathbf{x _0 \) sampled from a distribution \( p_0 \), SGM constructs a forward diffusion process \( \{ \mathbf{x _t \}_{t=0 ^1 \), which transfers \( \mathbf{x _0 \) into a terminal distribution \( p_1 \) via the forward SDE as \begin{equation}\mathrm{d} \mathbf{x}_t=f_t \mathrm{d} t+g_t \mathrm{d} \mathbf{w}_t, \label{SDE}\end{equation} where \( f_t \) is the drift function of \( \mathbf{x _t\) and \( g_t \) is a scalar function known as the diffusion coefficient.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: This process can be reversed by the following reverse-time SDE: \begin{equation}\mathrm{d} \mathbf{x}_t=\left[f_t-g_t^{2} \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_{t}(\mathbf{x})\right] \mathrm{d} t+g_t \mathrm{d} \mathbf{\bar w}_t, \label{related-work reverse sde}\end{equation} where \( \nabla_{\mathbf{x \log p_t(\mathbf{x ) \) denote the score function of \( p_t \), \( \mathbf{\bar w _t \) is the standard Wiener process when the time goes back to 0 from \( 1 \).}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}d x_t=\left[f_t-g_t^{2} \nabla_{x} \log p_{t}(x)\right] d t+g_t d \bar w_t, \label{related-work reverse sde}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-057: Then starting from samples of \( \mathbf{x _1 \sim p_1 \), we can obtain samples \( \mathbf{x _0 \sim p_0 \) through \eqnref{related-work reverse sde .}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Typically, the terminal distribution is set as a Gaussian distribution, i.e., \( \mathbf{x _1 \sim \mathcal{N (0, I) \), since it can be appropriately achieved by setting the drift function \( f_t \) as a linear function and the diffusion coefficient \( g_t \) as a scalar function such as in DDPM and Noise Conditional Score Network (NCSN).}}
elements.sent-2411-059: When the drift function \( f_t \) is set as a nonlinear function, the terminal distribution of SGM may no longer follow a simple Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-060: When the terminal distribution is an unknown data distribution, it is no longer possible to represent the diffusion process using a single linear SDE form.
elements.sent-2411-061: Instead, the problem extends to a system of linearly coupled SDE equations or the Schrödinger Bridge (SB) \cite{schrodinger1932theorie,leonard2013survey .}
elements.sent-2411-062: SB is a nonlinear extension of score-based diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-063: It defines the optimal transport between two arbitrary distributions, going beyond the limitation of Gaussian priors.
elements.sent-2411-064: The forward and backward SDEs of SB are as follows: \begin{equation}dx_t = \left[f_t + \beta_t \nabla \log \Psi(x_t, t)\right]dt + \sqrt{\beta_t} dw_t, \label{sb1} dx_t = \left[f_t - \beta_t \nabla \log \hat{\Psi}(x_t, t)\right]dt + \sqrt{\beta_t} d\overline{w}_t, \label{sb2}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{x _0 \sim p_0$ and $\mathbf{x _1 \sim p_1$ are drawn from boundary distributions in two distinct domains.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The functions \( \Psi, \hat{\Psi \in C^{2,1 (\mathbb{R ^d, [0, 1]) \) are time-varying energy potentials that solve the following coupled PDEs: \begin{equation}\left\{ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial \Psi(\mathbf{x},t)}{\partial t} = -\nabla \Psi^\top f - \frac{1}{2}\beta \Delta \Psi \frac{\partial \hat{\Psi}(\mathbf{x},t)}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot (\hat{\Psi} f) + \frac{1}{2}\beta \Delta \hat{\Psi} \end{aligned} \right. \label{pdf_sb}\end{equation} $\text{s.t.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: In contrast to the concise equations and computational efficiency of the SGM framework, the solving methods for SB are complex and computationally inefficient, due to the coupling constraints in \eqnref{pdf_sb , especially in high-dimensional settings \cite{chen2021likelihood,fernandes2022shooting .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: It remains unclear whether these methods can be effectively applied in practice for learning nonlinear diffusions.
elements.sent-2411-068: Therefore, practical techniques are needed to enhance the computational efficiency and applicability of SB.
elements.sent-2411-069: Recently, the Image-to-Image Schrödinger Bridge ($\mathbf{I ^2$SB) was proposed to learn the nonlinear diffusion using the same computation framework as SGM, which opens opportunities for using nonlinear diffusion models on image related tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-070: To make SB more compatible with the SGM framework, $\mathbf{I ^2$SB first reformulate Eq.(7) by setting \(\nabla_\mathbf{x \log\hat{\Psi (\mathbf{x _t, t) \) and \(\nabla_\mathbf{x \log \Psi(\mathbf{x _t, t) \) as the score function of the following linear SDEs, \begin{equation}dx_t = f_t dt + \sqrt{\beta_t} dw_t, \quad x_0 \sim \hat{\Psi}(\cdot, 0), \label{eq1} dx_t = f_t dt + \sqrt{\beta_t} d\overline{w}_t, \quad x_1 \sim \Psi(\cdot, 1). \label{eq2}\end{equation} However, the boundaries \( \hat{\Psi (\cdot, 0) \) and \( \Psi(\cdot, 1) \) remain intractable due to the coupling in \eqnref{pdf_sb .}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}dx_t = f_t dt + \sqrt{\beta_t} dw_t, \quad x_0 \sim \hat{\Psi}(\cdot, 0), \label{eq1} dx_t = f_t dt + \sqrt{\beta_t} d\overline{w}_t, \quad x_1 \sim \Psi(\cdot, 1). \label{eq2}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-071: To solve this, I\(^2\)SB assumes \( p_0(\cdot) := \delta_a(\cdot) \) to be the Dirac delta distribution.
elements.sent-2411-072: Then the initial distribution of \eqnref{eq1 and \eqref{eq2 can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\hat{\Psi}(\cdot, 0) = \delta_a(\cdot), \quad \Psi(\cdot, 1) = \frac{p_1}{\hat{\Psi}(\cdot, 1)}. \label{assume2}\end{equation} This eliminates the dependency on \( \Psi(\mathbf{x , 1) \) for solving \( \hat{\Psi (\mathbf{x , 1) \) and makes SB tractable.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: It worth noting that although the assumption of Dirac delta distribution may limit generalization beyond training samples, $\mathbf{I ^2$SB demonstrates that the score network generalizes well, partly due to the strong generalization capability of neural networks \cite{zhang2021understanding .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Based on the above theories, the SGM computational framework can be applied to SB without dealing with the intractability of the nonlinear drift.
elements.sent-2411-075: Specifically, the DDPM procedure is adopted for the training and sampling of SB.
elements.sent-2411-076: During training, given paired samples (\(\mathbf{x _0\), \(\mathbf{x _1\)), sampling \(\mathbf{x _t\) at an arbitrary timestep \(t\) can be formed as: \begin{equation}q(\mathbf{x}_t | \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t; \mu_t(\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1), \Sigma_t) \label{pertubation}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mu_t = \frac{\bar{\sigma}_t^2}{\bar{\sigma}_t^2 + \sigma_t^2} \mathbf{x}_0 + \frac{\sigma_t^2}{\bar{\sigma}_t^2 + \sigma_t^2} \mathbf{x}_1, \Sigma_t = \frac{\sigma_t^2 \bar{\sigma}_t^2}{\bar{\sigma}_t^2 + \sigma_t^2} \cdot I,\end{equation} where \(\sigma_t^2 := \int_0^t \beta_\tau d\tau\) and \(\bar{\sigma _t^2 := \int_t^1 \beta_\tau d\tau\) are variances accumulated from either side.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The training process is shown in \algoref{alg:training .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Training} \label{alg:training} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require clean $p_0(\cdot)$ and guided $p_1(\cdot|\mathbf{x}_0)$ datasets \Repeat \State $t \sim \mathcal{U}([0, 1])$, $\mathbf{x}_0 \sim p_0(\mathbf{x}_0)$, $\mathbf{x}_1 \sim p_1(\mathbf{x}_1|\mathbf{x}_0)$ \State $\mathbf{x}_t \sim q(\mathbf{x}_t | \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1)$ \State Take gradient descent step on $\left\lVert \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, t; \theta) - \frac{\mathbf{x}_t - \mathbf{x}_0}{\sigma_t} \right\lVert$ \Until{convergence} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-078: Based on \( t \in [0,1] \), and denoting the discrete time steps as \( 0 = t_0 < \cdots < t_n < \cdots < t_N = 1 \), we simplify by using \( n \) to represent \( t_n \).
elements.sent-2411-079: During the generation process, the recursive posterior discrete sampling using the trained parameterized mapping \( \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x _t, t; \theta) \mapsto \mathbf{x _0^\epsilon \) can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}_{n} \sim p(\mathbf{x}_{n} | \mathbf{x}_0^\epsilon, \mathbf{x}_{n+1}), \quad \mathbf{x}_N \sim p_1. \label{posterior}\end{equation}.}}

(b) In the reverse process, the learned noise function $\epsilon$ maps to $\mathbf{x}_0$, corrects it using undersampled data, and reconstructs a high-quality $\mathbf{x}_0$ from $\mathbf{x}_N$. Further quality improvements can be achieved through the inversion process. } \label{fig:general}
elements.sent-2411-081: The imaging model of MR reconstruction can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}+{\epsilon}, \label{MR forward model}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{y}$ is the undersampled measurements in the frequency domain~(i.e., $k$-space), $\mathbf{x}$ is the image to be reconstructed, $\mathbf{A}$ denotes the encoding matrix, $\mathbf{A =\mathbf{M}\cdot \mathbf{F \cdot \text{csm}$, $\mathbf{M}$ is the undersampling operator, $\mathbf{F}$ denotes the Fourier operator, $\text{csm}$ denotes the coil sensitivity, and ${\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N (0, \sigma^2_\epsilon)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Since \eqnref{MR forward model is an ill-posed problem, additional prior information is necessary to solve it.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Given a guiding image \(\mathbf{b}\), incorporating the structural similarity prior between the guiding image and the reconstructed image, the solution to \eqnref{MR forward model can be expressed as following constrained optimization problem: \begin{equation}\min_{\mathbf{x}} R(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{b}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}, \label{MR optimization problem}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{b}$ is the guiding image, and $R(\mathbf{x , \mathbf{b )$ represents the structural similarity prior between $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{b}$, such as group wavelet sparsity \cite{huang2014fast and weighted similarity of intensity \cite{weizman2016reference .}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\min_{x} R(x,b) \quad subject to \quad y = Ax, \label{MR optimization problem}\end{equation}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{Guided reconstruction} \label{alg:ddpm_guide} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require $\mathbf{x}_N \sim p_1$, trained $\epsilon(\cdot, \cdot; \theta)$, undersampled data $\mathbf{y}$ \For{$n = N$ to $1$} \State Predict $\mathbf{x}_0^\epsilon$ using $\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_n, t_n; \theta)$ \State CG based data consistency mapping\; \State $\mathbf{x}_{n-1} \sim p(\mathbf{x}_{n-1} | \mathbf{x}_0^\epsilon, \mathbf{x}_n)$ \EndFor \State return $\mathbf{x}_0$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-100: Under this condition, the posterior sampling process in \eqnref{posterior reduces to: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}_n = \frac{\alpha_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} \mathbf{x}_0 + \frac{\sigma_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} \mathbf{x}_{n+1} \label{flow}\end{equation} Subsequently, to diffuse \(\mathbf{x _n\) into \(\mathbf{x _{n+1}\), we solve for \(\mathbf{x _0\) using the denoising mapping from \eqnref{loss and apply it to the affine transformation form of \eqnref{flow : \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{x}_n + \frac{\alpha_n^2 \sigma_{n+1}}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_{n+1}, n+1; \theta)\end{equation} However, during the inversion process, the predicted noise at time \( n+1 \) is unknown at time \( n \).}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-107: We conducted experiments on a paired T1- and FLAIR T2-weighted image dataset, which includes fully sampled k-space data from 27 healthy volunteers.
elements.sent-2411-108: The local institutional review board approved the experiments, and informed consent was obtained from each volunteer.
elements.sent-2411-109: All data were obtained using a 3T MR scanner (uMR 790, United Imaging Healthcare, China) with a 32-channel head coil.
elements.sent-2411-110: The T1-weighted images were acquired by a 3D GRE sequence, and the FLAIR T2-weighted images were acquired using a 3D FSE sequence.
elements.sent-2411-111: For each volunteer, the position and spatial resolution were the same for both sequences with acquisition matrix = $240 \times 240 \times 176$ (RO × PE1 × PE2) and field of view (FOV) = $240 \times 240 \times 176 \, \text{mm ^3$.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Other imaging parameters were as follows.
elements.sent-2411-113: For the FLAIR-T2 sequence: $\text{TR/TE = 6000/396.4 \, \text{ms}$, inversion time = 1825 ms, echo train length = 240, bandwidth = 600 Hz/Pixel, and elliptical scanning was used.}
elements.sent-2411-115: For the T1 sequence: $\text{TR/TE = 7.7/3 \, \text{ms}$, flip angle = 9, echo train length = 176, and bandwidth = 250 Hz/Pixel.}
elements.sent-2411-116: The scan time was 8 minutes and 36 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-117: The 3D k-space data were divided into 2D slices along the RO direction by applying the inverse Fourier transform in that direction.
elements.sent-2411-118: The first 25 and last 50 slices, which contained only background, were removed from the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-119: Then coil compression was applied to compress the data to 18 channels to reduce computational load \cite{zhang2013coil .}
elements.sent-2411-120: Zero-padding was applied to increase the image size to $256 \times 256$, facilitating network operations.
elements.sent-2411-121: The k-space data of T1 and T2-weighted image was retrospectively undersampled with net acceleration factors ($R$) of 11.2 and 14.4.
elements.sent-2411-122: The CAIPI undersampling scheme \cite{breuer2005controlled was employed with a $48 \times 48$ k-space center fully sampled.}
elements.sent-2411-123: The coil sensitivity maps were estimated using the fully sampled k-space center with the ESPIRiT algorithm \cite{uecker2014eSPIRiT .}

elements.sent-2411-159: For such cases, approximate solutions can be achieved by appropriately designing inverse problems or by optimizing with a specific loss function via gradient descent.
elements.sent-2411-160: In summary, \( I^2 \)SB-inversion extends to diverse applications with known or approximate forward operators, achieving high-precision multi-modal image translation by aligning guiding and target image structures.
elements.sent-2411-161: This study incorporates a inversion strategy into guided reconstruction to correct structural differences, but this is limited to correcting the differences from the data perspective, without enhancing the parametric denoising mapping capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-162: For mapping enhancement, our future work will focus on two aspects:.
elements.sent-2411-163: Adaptive fine-tuning of weights during reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-164: The mapping weights are fine-tuned based on a corrected single paired sample, and then a weighted combination with the initial weights is used as the final mapping.
elements.sent-2411-165: Feature layer embedding optimization of the mapping based on corrected data.
elements.sent-2411-166: Features from the corrected data are introduced into the mapping network decoder layer via an attention mechanism as guidance during reconstruction, reducing uncertainty to enhance the mapping.
elements.sent-2411-167: In this paper, we propose a novel guided reconstruction framework based on the Schrödinger Bridge, named $\mathbf{I ^2$SB-inversion.}
elements.sent-2411-168: To address the challenge of accommodating structural differences in multi-contrast images, we introduce an innovative inversion strategy from image editing, enabling correction of the guiding image to enhance structural alignment.
elements.sent-2411-169: Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly improves reconstruction accuracy and stability on the paired T1 and T2-FLAIR dataset, confirming its effectiveness and superiority.
elements.sent-2411-170: This study was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grant nos.
elements.sent-2411-171: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is an inherently multi-contrast modality, where cross-contrast priors can be exploited to improve image reconstruction from undersampled data.
elements.sent-2411-172: Recently, diffusion models have shown remarkable performance in MRI reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-173: However, they still struggle to effectively utilize such priors, mainly because existing methods rely on feature-level fusion in image or latent spaces, which lacks explicit structural correspondence and thus leads to suboptimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-174: To address this issue, we propose $\mathbf{I ^2$SB-Inversion, a multi-contrast guided reconstruction framework based on the Schrödinger Bridge (SB).}
elements.sent-2411-175: The proposed method performs pixel-wise translation between paired contrasts, providing explicit structural constraints between the guidance and target images.
elements.sent-2411-176: Furthermore, an Inversion strategy is introduced to correct inter-modality misalignment, which often occurs in guided reconstruction, thereby mitigating artifacts and improving reconstruction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-177: Experiments on paired T1- and T2-weighted datasets demonstrate that $\mathbf{I ^2$SB-Inversion achieves a high acceleration factor of up to 14.4× and consistently outperforms existing methods in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations.}
elements.sent-2411-178: A widely adopted strategy to mitigate this limitation is k-space undersampling, followed by image reconstruction that leverages prior information.
elements.sent-2411-179: Typical priors include sparsity and low-rankness of the target images in either image or transform domains, as well as deep priors learned from historical images.
elements.sent-2411-180: Such priors have been extensively explored in compressed sensing (CS) \cite{bilgic2011multi, zhao2012image,cgspirit and deep learning (DL) \cite{zhang2018ista,cui2021equilibrated,modl, gungor2023adaptive,cui2023spirit,jiang2024ppn,vn -based reconstruction methods, leading to significant improvements in reconstruction quality.}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Beyond these priors, another important source of prior information can be derived from other MR contrast images of the same subject \cite{gong2015promise .}
elements.sent-2411-182: These images preserve consistent anatomical structures and can help mitigate the degradation caused by undersampling.
elements.sent-2411-183: Approaches exploiting such multi-contrast priors can be broadly classified into two categories: joint reconstruction and guided reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-184: Joint reconstruction simultaneously reconstructs all multi-contrast images from undersampled k-space data by leveraging their correlations.
elements.sent-2411-185: It is commonly used in imaging techniques inherently designed for multi-contrast acquisitions, such as quantitative MRI \cite{bian2024diffusion and contrast-enhanced dynamic imaging \cite{van1993keyhole, hess1999maximum, lingala2011accelerated,liang1994efficient,mistretta2006highly .}}
elements.sent-2411-186: In these techniques, all contrast images are acquired using a single sequence, usually with the same acceleration factor but different sampling masks, which provides complementary information across images.
elements.sent-2411-187: The priors from multi-contrasts can be jointly modeled for image reconstruction, for example, through joint sparsity \cite{kopanoglu2020simultaneous,trzasko2011group,guo2023joint , joint low-rankness \cite{haldar2013improved, bustin2019high,bilgic2018improving , and joint learning-based priors including joint variational networks (JVN) \cite{polak2020joint , dual-domain reconstruction networks\cite{dual_domain , and information-sharing networks\cite{sun2019deep .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-188: However, lacking high-quality references, joint reconstruction methods struggle to achieve high-fidelity reconstruction when the number of available contrasts is limited.
elements.sent-2411-189: As a result, their performance is constrained when applied to conventional single-contrast images, such as T1- and T2-weighted imaging.
elements.sent-2411-190: In contrast, guided reconstruction improves the reconstruction of the target image by introducing a high-quality guidance image as a structural prior.
elements.sent-2411-191: These approaches can be grouped into anatomical prior-based methods \cite{qu2014magnetic,fessler1992regularized,haldar2008anatomically,somayajula2010pet , deep learning-based methods\cite{yang2020model,Dar2020 , and multi-modality extensions\cite{knoll2016joint , where the guidance image is either fully sampled or reconstructed from lightly undersampled data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Owing to the high reliability of the guidance image, it offers a more flexible strategy than joint reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-193: In recent years, diffusion models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in MRI reconstruction and achieved state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance \cite{scoremri,peng2022towards .}
elements.sent-2411-194: Despite their advances, only a limited number of studies have explored their application in guided reconstruction due to challenges in effectively integrating priors from the guidance image to the target image.
elements.sent-2411-195: Typically, the guidance prior is incorporated through feature-level fusion in the image or latent space.
elements.sent-2411-196: However, lacking explicit structural correspondence, these approaches impose only weak constraints between the guidance and target images, thereby limiting their performance.
elements.sent-2411-197: To address this limitation, we introduce a nonlinear extension of diffusion models—Schrodinger Bridge (SB) \cite{liu20232, kim2023unpaired,mirza2024super,hu2024adobi for diffusion-based guided reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Unlike conventional diffusion models that start from a Gaussian distribution, SB enables general transformations between arbitrary data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-199: Moreover, it allows for pixel-level translation between paired images, which offers an explicit structural constraint between the guidance and target images.
elements.sent-2411-200: Therefore, it may overcome the limitations of feature-level fusion and improve the performance of guided reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-201: Another challenge in guided reconstruction is anatomical misalignment across multi-contrast images \cite{LAI201795,9288664,LAI201693 , which arises from subject motion or differences in tissue contrast between imaging modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-202: Such misalignment may introduce spurious structures and artifacts in the reconstructed image.
elements.sent-2411-203: To address this, we incorporate an Inversion strategy inspired by image editing techniques \cite{huberman2024edit , which reverses the generative process to recover latent variables from a known image.}
elements.sent-2411-204: In our SB-based guided reconstruction, Inversion is used to identify the guiding variable along the SB trajectory that best corresponds to the target image, correcting reconstruction errors caused by misalignment between multi-contrast images.
elements.sent-2411-205: In this work, we propose a novel SB-based guided reconstruction framework with Inversion to correct spatial misalignment between multi-contrast images.
elements.sent-2411-206: First, an initial target image is generated by translating the guidance image through SB sampling while enforcing data consistency with the acquired k-space.
elements.sent-2411-207: This image then serves as the starting point for Inversion to infer a structurally aligned guidance variable, which is subsequently used for a second round of SB sampling to obtain the final reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-208: The main contributions are: 1) We propose a Schrodinger Bridge (SB)-based framework for guided MRI reconstruction, enabling pixel level cross-modality mapping.
elements.sent-2411-209: SGM\cite{ho2020denoising,song2019generative,song2020score is a general framework for diffusion models that describes the diffusion process as the solution of stochastic differential equations (SDEs).}
elements.sent-2411-210: Given an initial state \( \mathbf{x _0 \) sampled from a distribution \( p_0 \), SGM constructs a forward diffusion process \( \{ \mathbf{x _t \}_{t=0 ^1 \), which gradually transfers \( \mathbf{x _0 \) into a Gaussian distribution via the forward SDE\cite{song2020score as: \begin{equation}\mathrm{d} \mathbf{x}_t=f_t \mathrm{d} t+g_t \mathrm{d} \mathbf{w}_t, \label{SDE}\end{equation} where \( f_t \) is the drift function of \( \mathbf{x _t\) and \( g_t \) is a scalar function known as the diffusion coefficient.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-211: A concrete example is the Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM)\cite{ho2020denoising , which can be viewed as a discrete-time approximation of the Variance Preserving SDE (VP-SDE), the continuous formulation of this process, with: \begin{equation}f_t = -\frac{1}{2} \beta(t) \mathbf{x}_t, \quad g_t = \sqrt{\beta(t)},\end{equation} where $\beta(t)$ is a monotonically increasing noise schedule.}
elements.sent-2411-212: This design ensures a smooth transition from the data distribution to a Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-213: In contrast, the forward process can be reversed by the following reverse-time SDE\cite{song2020score from a Gaussian distribution to a target distribution: \begin{equation}\mathrm{d} \mathbf{x}_t=\left[f_t-g_t^{2} \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_{t}(\mathbf{x})\right] \mathrm{d} t+g_t \mathrm{d} \mathbf{\bar w}_t, \label{related-work reverse sde}\end{equation} where \( \nabla_{\mathbf{x \log p_t(\mathbf{x ) \) denotes the score function of \( p_t \), and \( \mathbf{\bar w _t \) is the standard Wiener process when time goes backward from $t=1$ to $t=0$\cite{song2019generative .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: Then starting from Gaussian noise, we can obtain samples \( \mathbf{x _0 \sim p_0 \) through Eq.~\eqref{related-work reverse sde .}}
elements.sent-2411-215: SB is a nonlinear extension of score-based diffusion models that enables transformations between two arbitrary data distributions~\cite{schrodinger1932theorie,leonard2013survey,chen2021likelihood,fernandes2022shooting .}
elements.sent-2411-216: Unlike conventional diffusion models\cite{ho2020denoising,song2019generative that are initialized from Gaussian noise with no structural information, SB can be initialized from related images that retain meaningful structural features.}
elements.sent-2411-217: This relaxation of the Gaussian prior assumption allows SB to better exploit available prior information and is particularly suited for conditional generation~\cite{chen2021likelihood,fernandes2022shooting .}
elements.sent-2411-218: A recent work in MRI reconstruction, i.e., the Fourier-constrained diffusion bridge (FDB)~\cite{fdb , adapts SB to directly learn the mapping between undersampled and fully sampled data and demonstrates its benefit.}
elements.sent-2411-219: In guided reconstruction, SB offers a direct and convenient way to translate between guidance and target images by learning the mapping between their distributions.
elements.sent-2411-220: However, the original SB model is computationally complex and difficult to apply in practice.
elements.sent-2411-221: Therefore, we employ a more practical variant developed for image-related tasks, namely image-to-image SB ($\mathrm{I^2SB}$)\cite{liu20232 in our guided reconstruction framework.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Specifically, given $\mathbf{x _0 \sim p_{\text{tar }$ and $\mathbf{x _N \sim p_{\text{guid }$, where $p_{\text{tar }$ and $p_{\text{guid }$ denote the distributions of the target and guidance images, respectively, the forward process can be formed as: \begin{equation}q(\mathbf{x}_n|\mathbf{x}_0,\mathbf{x}_N) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n;\,\mu_n(\mathbf{x}_0,\mathbf{x}_N),\Sigma_n), \label{forward_pro}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\mu_n = \frac{\bar{\sigma}_n^2}{\bar{\sigma}_n^2 + \sigma_n^2}\mathbf{x}_0 + \frac{\sigma_n^2}{\bar{\sigma}_n^2 + \sigma_n^2}\mathbf{x}_N, \quad \Sigma_n = \frac{\eta^2 \sigma_n^2\bar{\sigma}_n^2}{\bar{\sigma}_n^2 + \sigma_n^2}I,\end{equation} where $\sigma_n^2 = \sum_{i=0 ^{n \beta_i$ and $\bar{\sigma _n^2 = \sum_{i=n+1 ^{N \beta_i$ denote the accumulated noise variances from each side, $\beta_i$ represents the noise schedule, and $\eta \geq 0$ is a variance scaling parameter.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: In the training phase, given a training image pair $(\mathbf{x _0, \mathbf{x _N)$, the intermediate state $\mathbf{x _n$ at time step $n$ is sampled according to Eq.~\eqref{forward_pro .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: Then the residual predictor $\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x _n, n; \theta)$ is trained to predict the residual noise in $\mathbf{x _n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-225: The training algorithm is illustrated in Alg.~\ref{alg:training .}
elements.sent-2411-226: In the generation phase, the posterior sampling between adjacent steps can be derived using the Chapman-Kolmogorov~\cite{schrodinger1932theorie,leonard2013survey relation as: \begin{equation}q(x_n \mid x_0, x_N) = \int p(x_n \mid x_0, x_{n+1})\, q(x_{n+1} \mid x_0, x_N)\, dx_{n+1}. \label{eq:ck_relation}\end{equation} Since both $p$ and $q$ follow Gaussian distributions, the above integration also yields a Gaussian form.}
elements.formula-2411-024: \begin{equation}q(x_n \mid x_0, x_N) = \int p(x_n \mid x_0, x_{n+1})\, q(x_{n+1} \mid x_0, x_N)\, dx_{n+1}. \label{eq:ck_relation}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-227: By matching the mean and variance terms, the closed-form expression of the posterior sampling can be obtained as: \begin{equation}p(x_{n} \midx_0 , x_{n+1}) = N\left(x_{n}; \frac{\alpha_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} x_0 + \frac{\sigma_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} x_{n+1}, \right. \nonumber \quad \left. \frac{ \eta^2 \sigma_n^2 \alpha_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} \cdot I\right), \label{posterior_clean}\end{equation} where $\alpha_n^2 = \sigma_{n+1 ^2-\sigma_{n ^2 = \beta_n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-228: As $\mathbf{x _0$ is unavailable, $\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x _n, n; \theta)$ is used to predict the residual noise and derive an approximation of the target image denoted as $\mathbf{x _0^\epsilon$, which is then used for posterior sampling: \begin{equation}p(x_{n} \midx_0^\epsilon, x_{n+1}) = N\left(x_{n}; \frac{\alpha_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} x_0^\epsilon + \frac{\sigma_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} x_{n+1}, \right. \nonumber \quad \left. \frac{ \eta^2 \sigma_n^2 \alpha_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} \cdot I\right). \label{posterior}\end{equation} Based on the above derivation, the generation process begins with $\mathbf{x _N$ and iteratively performs residual prediction, image generation, and posterior sampling, allowing progressive refinement toward high-quality image translation.}}}}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: [H] \caption{Training} \label{alg:training} Input: $p_\text{tar}$: target distribution $p_\text{guid}$: guided distribution $\epsilon_\theta(\cdot, \cdot; \theta)$: residual predictor [0.3em] Output: Trained parameters $\theta$ \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Repeat \State Sample $n \sim \mathcal{U}([0, N])$, $\mathbf{x}_0 \sim p_\text{tar}(\mathbf{x}_0)$, $\mathbf{x}_N \sim p_\text{guid}(\mathbf{x}_N|\mathbf{x}_0)$ \State Sample $\mathbf{x}_n \sim q(\mathbf{x}_n | \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_N)$ \State Take a gradient descent step on \[ \left\lVert \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_n, n; \theta) - \frac{\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{x}_0}{\sigma_n} \right\rVert \] \Until{convergence} \end{algorithmic}
elements.figure-2411-009: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/sb_framework.pdf}} \caption{(a) Forward step: The target image $\mathbf{x}_0$ gradually transforms into the guidance image $\mathbf{x}_N$, forming the SB trajectory $\{\mathbf{x}_0, \dots, \mathbf{x}_N\}$. Paired data $(\mathbf{x}_n$, $\mathbf{x}_0)$ are then used to train the denoiser $\epsilon_\theta$. (b) Backward step: Starting from $\mathbf{x}_N$, the target image $\mathbf{x}_0$ is reconstructed through iterative sampling, with data consistency enforced at each step to match the acquired k-space data. (c) Inversion step: After (b), $\mathbf{x}_0$ is converted into the aligned guidance image $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$, followed by resampling to obtain the final reconstruction $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_0$.} \label{fig:general}
elements.sent-2411-229: The imaging model of MR reconstruction can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}+\boldsymbol{\xi}, \label{MR forward model}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{y}$ is the undersampled k-space data, $\mathbf{x}$ is the image to be reconstructed, $\mathbf{A}$ denotes the encoding matrix, $\mathbf{A =\mathbf{M}\cdot \mathbf{F \cdot \text{csm}$, $\mathbf{M}$ is the undersampling operator, $\mathbf{F}$ denotes the Fourier operator, $\text{csm}$ denotes the coil sensitivity, and ${\boldsymbol{\xi \sim \mathcal{N (0, \sigma^2_{\boldsymbol{\xi )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-230: Since Eq.~\eqref{MR forward model is an ill-posed problem, additional prior information is necessary to solve it.}
elements.sent-2411-231: Given a guidance image \(\mathbf{b}\), incorporating the structural similarity prior between the guidance image and the reconstructed image, the solution to Eq.~\eqref{MR forward model can be expressed as the following constrained optimization problem: \begin{equation}\min_{\mathbf{x}} R(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{b}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}, \label{MR optimization problem}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{b}$ is the guidance image, and $R(\mathbf{x , \mathbf{b )$ represents the structural similarity prior between $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{b}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-232: In traditional methods, the optimization problem is often decoupled into two alternately optimized subproblems to obtain the optimal solution.
elements.algorithm-2411-005: [H] \caption{I$^2$SB-Recon} \label{alg:ddpm_guide} Input: $\mathbf{b} \sim p_{\text{guid}}$: \text{guidance image} $\epsilon_\theta(\cdot, \cdot; \theta)$: residual predictor $\mathbf{y}$: undersampled k-space data Output: $\mathbf{x}_0$: Reconstructed image \begin{algorithmic}[1] $\mathbf{x}_N=\mathbf{b}$ \For{$n = N-1,\dots,1,0$} \State $\mathbf{x}_0^{\epsilon} = \mathbf{x}_n - \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_n, n; \theta)\, \sigma_n$ \Comment{estimate target image} \State $\mathbf{\bar{x}}_0^\epsilon=\text{CG}(\mathbf{x}_0^\epsilon,\mathbf{y})$ \Comment{enforce k-space data consistency} \State Sample $\mathbf{x}_{n} \sim p(\mathbf{x}_{n} \mid \mathbf{\bar{x}}_0^\epsilon, \mathbf{x}_{n+1})$ \EndFor \State return $\mathbf{x}_0$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.subsection-2411-014: Guided Reconstruction Based on Schrödinger Bridge
elements.sent-2411-233: The regularizer $R(\mathbf{x , \mathbf{b )$ can be interpreted as the evolution path between the distributions of target and guidance images, denoted as $p_{\text{tar }$ and $p_{\text{guid }$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: Within the SB framework, this path can be formalized as a probabilistic process $\{\mathbf{x _n\}_{n=0 ^N$ that evolves from $p_{\text{tar }$ to $p_{\text{guid }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-235: In the reverse process, following the framework of $\mathrm{I^2SB}$, a network $\epsilon_\theta$ is used to predict the residual noise $\|\mathbf{x _n - \mathbf{x _0\|$ from $\mathbf{x _n$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: This network $\epsilon_\theta$ is trained according to Alg.~\ref{alg:training using paired guidance and target MR image datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-237: After training, the target image can be reconstructed through iterative sampling $\{\mathbf{x _n\}$, $n = N \to 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-238: Starting from $n = N$ and $\mathbf{x _N = \mathbf{b}$, the initial estimation of $\mathbf{x _0$ can be represented by \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}_0^{\epsilon} = \mathbf{x}_n - \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_n, n; \theta)\, \sigma_n. \label{eq:x0_estimation}\end{equation} Then, the data consistency constraint in Eq.~\eqref{MR optimization problem should be applied to ensure that the generated image is consistent with the acquired k-space data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-239: Accordingly, Eq.~\eqref{MR optimization problem can be reformulated as an optimization problem, \begin{equation}\min_{\mathbf{x}} \|\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}\|_2^2 + \lambda \|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^\epsilon_0\|_2^2, \label{MR reverse problem}\end{equation} where $\lambda$ is the regularization parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Various methods can be used to solve this optimization problem, including the projection method~\cite{griswold2002generalized , gradient descent method~\cite{lustig2007sparse , and conjugate gradient (CG)~\cite{pruessmann1999sense .}}}
elements.sent-2411-241: Previous studies~\cite{chung2023decomposed,zhu2023denoising have demonstrated that, under the assumption that the tangent space of the denoised sample $\mathbf{x _0^{\epsilon}$ can be represented as a Krylov subspace, using the standard CG method ensures that the optimization direction aligns with the gradient direction of the distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-242: This property keeps the reconstructed image well confined within the desired distribution $p_{\text{tar }$.}
elements.sent-2411-243: Therefore, we employ the CG method to solve the optimization problem in this study.
elements.sent-2411-244: Its solution is denoted as \begin{equation}\mathbf{\bar{x}}_0^\epsilon=CG(x_0^{\epsilon}, y). \label{correct}\end{equation} Since the CG correction remains on $p_{\text{tar }$ and satisfies the SB prior, iterations along the SB path can be performed to progressively refine the reconstructed image.}
elements.sent-2411-245: We employ posterior sampling to map the CG-corrected sample back onto the SB path for the next iteration: \begin{equation}p(x_{n} \mid \mathbf{\bar{x}}_0^\epsilon, x_{n+1}) = N\left(x_{n}; \frac{\alpha_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} , \mathbf{\bar{x}}_0^\epsilon + \frac{\sigma_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} x_{n+1}, \right. \nonumber \ \quad \left. \frac{\eta^2 \sigma_n^2 \alpha_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} \cdot I \right). \label{correct}\end{equation} \algoref{alg:ddpm_guide illustrates the reconstruction algorithm, termed $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Recon.}}
elements.sent-2411-246: In guided reconstruction, the guidance and target images are typically assumed to be perfectly aligned in the spatial domain.
elements.sent-2411-247: However, this assumption often fails in real-world scenarios due to subject motion during scanning, leading to misalignment between multi-contrast images and degradation in the reconstructed image quality.
elements.sent-2411-248: To mitigate this issue, an Inversion strategy is introduced.
elements.sent-2411-249: Specifically, using the reconstructed image $\mathbf{x _0$ obtained by I$^2$SB-Recon as an initialization, the Inverse process samples along the SB trajectory toward the guidance distribution to infer a corresponding sample $\hat{\mathbf{b }$, followed by re-reconstructing the image with $\hat{\mathbf{b }$ as the new guidance.}}}
elements.sent-2411-250: Since $\mathbf{x _0$ has been partially corrected by the acquired data, $\hat{\mathbf{b }$ is expected to be more spatially aligned with the true target image than the original guidance image $\mathbf{b}$, thereby alleviating reconstruction errors caused by misalignment.}}
elements.sent-2411-251: In the Inversion process, a deterministic Probability Flow ODE is adopted instead of the SDE to avoid introducing additional randomness.
elements.sent-2411-252: By setting $\eta = 0$ in Eq.~\eqref{posterior_clean , the posterior sampling process degenerates into an ODE, simplifying the sampling procedure as follows: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}_{n} = \frac{\alpha_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} \mathbf{x}_0 + \frac{\sigma_n^2}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} \mathbf{x}_{n+1}, \label{flow}\end{equation} $\mathbf{x _0$ in the above formula can be approximated using Eq.~\eqref{eq:x0_estimation at step $n\!+\!1$, i.e. $\mathbf{x _0^{\epsilon = \mathbf{x _{n+1 - \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x _{n+1 , n+1; \theta)\, \sigma_{n+1}$, and Eq.~\eqref{flow can then be reformulated as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{x}_n + \frac{\alpha_n^2 \sigma_{n+1}}{\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} \, \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_{n+1}, n+1; \theta). \label{flow1}\end{equation} However, during the opposite flow (Inversion process), the residual part $\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x _{n+1 , n+1; \theta)$ is unknown at time step \( n \) when inference from $n$ to $n+1$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-253: A feasible solution is to use $\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x _{n , n; \theta)$ as a substitute.}}
elements.sent-2411-254: To achieve this, we reconsider the marginal distribution $q(\mathbf{x _n \mid \mathbf{x _0, \mathbf{x _N)$, which can also be simplified as: \begin{equation}x_{n} = \frac{\bar{\sigma}_n^2}{\bar{\sigma}_n^2 + \sigma_n^2}x_0 + \frac{\sigma_n^2}{\bar{\sigma}_n^2 + \sigma_n^2}x_N, \label{flow_marginal}\end{equation} $\mathbf{x _n = \mathbf{x _0^{\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x _n, n; \theta)\, \sigma_n$ can be derived from Eq.~\eqref{eq:x0_estimation .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-255: Then it can be combined with Eq.~\eqref{flow_marginal to obtain the following formula: \begin{equation}\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_{n}, n; \theta) = -\frac{\sigma_n}{\bar{\sigma}_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} \mathbf{x}_0^\epsilon + \frac{\sigma_n}{\bar{\sigma}_n^2 + \sigma_n^2} \mathbf{x}_N. \label{sample}\end{equation} Connecting Eq.~\eqref{sample with different time steps $n+1$ and $n$, the relationship between the predicted noises can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_{n+1}, n+1; \theta) = \frac{\sigma_{n+1} }{\sigma_n} \cdot \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_n, n; \theta). \label{relation between noise}\end{equation} By substituting Eq.~\eqref{relation between noise into Eq.~\eqref{flow1 , a feasible formulation for the Inversion strategy can be derived as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{x}_n + \frac{\alpha_n^2 \sigma_{n+1}^2}{\sigma_n (\alpha_n^2 + \sigma_n^2)} \cdot \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_n, n; \theta), \label{eq:inversion}\end{equation} Through Eq.~\eqref{eq:inversion , the Inversion process can be formalized using $\{\mathbf{x '_n\}$, $n = 0 \rightarrow N$, where $\mathbf{x '_0$ is reconstructed using I$^2$SB-Recon.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-256: The pseudocode of I$^2$SB-Inversion is presented in ~\algoref{alg:highSNR .}
elements.algorithm-2411-006: [H] \caption{I$^2$SB-Inversion } \label{alg:highSNR} Input: $\mathbf{b} \sim p_{\text{guid}}$: guidance image $\epsilon_\theta(\cdot, \cdot; \theta)$: residual predictor $\mathbf{y}$: undersampled k-space data Output: $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_0$: reconstructed image \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Run Alg.~\ref{alg:ddpm_guide} to obtain $\mathbf{x}'_0= \mathbf{x}_0$ \State Inversion process: \For{$n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$} \State $\mathbf{x}'_{n+1} = \text{Inversion}(\mathbf{x}'_n)$ using Eq.~\eqref{eq:inversion} \EndFor \State $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \mathbf{x}'_N$ \State Run Alg.~\ref{alg:ddpm_guide} starting from $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ to obtain final $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_0$ \State return $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_0$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-257: We conducted experiments on two datasets comprising paired T1- and T2-weighted images acquired on a 3T MR scanner (uMR 790, United Imaging Healthcare, China): a knee dataset and a brain dataset.
elements.sent-2411-258: All experiments were approved by the local institutional review board.
elements.sent-2411-259: The brain dataset includes fully sampled k-space data from 29 healthy volunteers using a 32-channel head coil.
elements.sent-2411-260: T1-weighted images were acquired with a 3D GRE sequence, and corresponding T2-FLAIR images with a 3D FSE sequence.
elements.sent-2411-261: For each volunteer, both sequences had identical positioning and spatial resolution, with acquisition matrix = 240 $\times$ 240 $\times$ 176 and FOV = 240 $\times$ 240 $\times$ 176 mm$^3$.
elements.sent-2411-262: Imaging parameters were as follows: For the T1 sequence: TR/TE = 7.7/3 ms, flip angle = 9°, echo train length = 176, bandwidth = 250 Hz/pixel, scan time = 8 min 36 s; For the T2 sequence: TR/TE = 6000/396.4 ms, echo train length = 240, bandwidth = 600 Hz/pixel, elliptical scanning, scan time = 14 min.
elements.sent-2411-263: The knee dataset includes fully sampled k-space data from 13 healthy volunteers using a 12-channel knee coil.
elements.sent-2411-264: Both T1- and T2-weighted images were acquired using the 3D FSE sequence with acquisition matrix = $240 \times 240 \times 140$ and FOV = $160 \times 160 \times 150~\mathrm{mm ^3$.}
elements.sent-2411-265: Imaging parameters were as follows: For the T1 sequence, TR/TE = $600/15.36~\mathrm{ms}$, echo train length = $50$, bandwidth = $450~\mathrm{Hz/pixel}$, scan time = $4~\mathrm{min ~31~\mathrm{s}$; for the T2 sequence, TR/TE = $1400/259.2~\mathrm{ms}$, echo train length = $120$, bandwidth = $400~\mathrm{Hz/pixel}$, scan time = $9~\mathrm{min ~46~\mathrm{s}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-266: The 3D k-space data were divided into 2D slices along the RO direction by applying the inverse Fourier transform.
elements.sent-2411-267: For both the brain and knee datasets, background-only slices were discarded—specifically, the first 25 and last 50 slices for the brain data, and the first and last 48 slices for the knee data.
elements.sent-2411-268: Then, coil compression was used to compress the data to 18 channels to reduce computational load \cite{zhang2013coil .}
elements.sent-2411-269: Zero-padding was applied to increase the image size to 256 $\times$ 256, facilitating network operations.
elements.sent-2411-270: In our experiments, the T1-weighted image served as guidance for reconstructing the corresponding T2-weighted image.
elements.sent-2411-271: During training, both T1- and T2-weighted images were fully sampled, whereas during testing, the guidance T1-weighted images remained fully sampled, and the T2-weighted images were retrospectively undersampled with net acceleration factors (R) of 11.2 and 14.4.
elements.sent-2411-272: Note that undersampling masks were defined on the zero-padded 256 $\times$ 256 k-space, and R was computed with respect to this size.
elements.sent-2411-273: For the brain dataset, 23 volunteers were randomly selected for the training set, yielding 4163 matched image pairs, while the remaining 6 volunteers formed the test set, comprising 1086 matched image pairs.
elements.sent-2411-274: For the knee dataset, 10 volunteers were randomly selected for the training set, yielding 1600 matched image pairs, while the remaining 3 volunteers formed the test set, comprising 480 matched image pairs.
elements.sent-2411-275: The CAIPI undersampling scheme \cite{breuer2005controlled was employed with a 48$\times$48 k-space center fully sampled.}
elements.sent-2411-276: The network $\epsilon(\mathbf{x _n, n; \theta)$ was implemented using a U-Net architecture in\cite{dhariwal2021diffusion , which is widely used in diffusion models.}}
elements.sent-2411-277: The network has 552 million parameters across 91 layers and is initialized using the publicly released ADM checkpoint trained on ImageNet 256×256 (\href{https://github.com/openai/guided-diffusion {official ADM checkpoint ).}}
elements.sent-2411-278: We refer readers to the original work for detailed configurations.
elements.sent-2411-279: Because MR data are complex-valued, the real and imaginary components were represented as two separate channels in the network.
elements.sent-2411-280: During the data consistency step, they were recombined into a complex form to perform Fourier transforms, thereby enforcing consistency with the acquired k-space data.
elements.sent-2411-281: Implementation details and source code for $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Inversion are available at: \url{https://github.com/zhyjSIAT/I2SB-Inversion .}}
elements.sent-2411-282: We compared the $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Inversion method with several SOTA approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-283: For guided reconstruction methods, we selected the unrolling-based method MD-DAN \cite{yang2020model , the conditional distribution learning method Con-DDPM (defined as a conditional DDPM guided by an image), the JVN \cite{polak2020joint (defined as a conditional VN guided by an image), as well as I$^2$SB-Recon (without Inversion) to compare their performance under different guidance paradigms.}}
elements.sent-2411-284: We also compared our method with four categories of direct reconstruction methods without guidance: (a) the traditional parallel imaging and compressed sensing method CG-SPIRIT \cite{cgspirit ; (b) unrolling-based methods, including ISTA-Net \cite{zhang2018ista and MoDL \cite{modl ; (c) diffusion-based methods, including DDPM \cite{ho2020denoising , ScoreMRI \cite{scoremri , AdaDiff \cite{gungor2023adaptive , and PPN \cite{jiang2024ppn ; and (d) the Schrödinger bridge–based method FDB \cite{fdb .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-285: The implementation details were as follows.
elements.sent-2411-286: For unrolling-based methods, ISTA-Net was trained with a learning rate of 0.0001 and a batch size of 8, whereas MD-DAN used a batch size of 2 with a learning rate of 0.001.
elements.sent-2411-287: MoDL was configured with the number of layers $N$ set to 5 and the number of iterations $K$ to 10.
elements.sent-2411-288: VN employed a network depth of 10 iterations, with a batch size of 10 and a learning rate of 0.001.
elements.sent-2411-289: For diffusion-based methods, DDPM was configured with $\beta_{\text{max = 0.02$ and $\beta_{\text{min = 0.0001$, and Con-DDPM adopted the same settings.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-291: ScoreMRI was configured with $\sigma_{\text{min = 0.01$ and $\sigma_{\text{max = 3.78$, using a batch size of 1 and a learning rate of $2 \times 10^{-4}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-292: PPN employed the same learning rate of $2 \times 10^{-4}$, with the noise range set to $\sigma_{\text{min = 0.0001$ and $\sigma_{\text{max = 0.02$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-293: For Schrödinger bridge methods, FDB was trained with a batch size of 1 and a learning rate of $1 \times 10^{-4}$.
elements.sent-2411-294: In The whole process, except the Inversion process, $\eta$ is set to 1.
elements.sent-2411-295: Three metrics were used to quantitatively evaluate the results, including normalized root mean squared error (NRMSE), peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), and structural similarity index (SSIM)\cite{SSIM .}
elements.sent-2411-296: Fig.~\ref{fig:Guided_knee shows the results of different guided reconstruction methods on the knee dataset with R = 11.2 and 14.4.}
elements.sent-2411-297: At R = 11.2, detail loss can be observed in the reconstructions from JVN, MD-DAN, and Con-DDPM, as indicated by the arrows.
elements.sent-2411-298: At the higher acceleration of R = 14.4, aliasing artifacts appear in the reconstructions of JVN, MD-DAN, and Con-DDPM, with particularly severe artifacts in Con-DDPM.
elements.sent-2411-299: In contrast, $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Recon achieves superior performance by effectively removing aliasing artifacts while preserving fine structural details.}

elements.sent-2411-312: In this study, we propose a guided MRI reconstruction framework, termed $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Inversion, which integrates the Schrödinger Bridge (SB) formulation with an inversion strategy to enhance structural fidelity in cross-modality reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-313: The proposed method establishes an explicit, pixel-level probabilistic mapping between the guidance and target domains through the SB framework, enabling direct learning of structural correspondences during the generative process.
elements.sent-2411-314: This formulation enables the preservation of detailed anatomical structures from the guidance image while maintaining the physical data consistency required by the reconstruction task.
elements.sent-2411-315: Moreover, the introduced inversion strategy alleviates cross-modality misalignment by searching for an approximately aligned guidance representation along the SB trajectory based on the initial reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-316: This process further enhances anatomical consistency and improves reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-317: Consequently, $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Inversion achieves high acceleration factors of up to 14.4× and outperforms existing SOTA methods.}
elements.sent-2411-318: In guided reconstruction, cross-modal misalignment often introduces artifacts and reduces reconstruction accuracy because the reconstructed image must satisfy constraints from both the guidance image and the undersampled data.
elements.sent-2411-319: In $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Inversion, the structural discrepancies introduced by the guidance image can be reduced through data consistency, yielding a partially corrected image.}
elements.sent-2411-320: Then this result is used as a starting point for inversion to infer a more structurally aligned guidance variable, which reinitializes the sampling process.
elements.sent-2411-321: Through this iterative correction, the guidance and target become progressively aligned, thereby mitigating misalignment artifacts and improving reconstruction performance.
elements.sent-2411-322: To assess the effect of the Inversion strategy, two scenarios were simulated with significant misalignment or motion between the guidance and target images.
elements.sent-2411-323: The first scenario simulated in-plane misalignment by translating and rotating the guidance image, where the translations and rotation angles were randomly sampled from Gaussian distributions with zero mean and standard deviations of 10 pixels and 10 degrees, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-324: The second scenario simulated through-plane misalignment by introducing slice offsets between the target and guidance images.
elements.sent-2411-325: For example, when reconstructing the 1st T2 slice, the 1st, 21st, 41st, and 61st T1 slices were used as guidance.
elements.sent-2411-326: Fig.~\ref{fig:random_move presents the reconstruction results of the in-plane misaligned data using $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Recon and $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Inversion at R = 14.4.}}}
elements.sent-2411-327: For through-plane misalignment, Fig.~\ref{fig:slice illustrates the variation of quantitative metrics of the reconstructed images with respect to slice offset.}
elements.sent-2411-328: The results show that $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Inversion exhibits only minor performance degradation as the slice offset increases, whereas the performance degrades significantly without the inversion strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-329: Furthermore, Fig.~\ref{fig:ablation presents the mean NRMSE, PSNR, and SSIM curves of 1086 brain slices reconstructed using $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Recon and $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Inversion with R = 11.2, 14.4, 18.9, 23.2, and 28.4.}}}
elements.sent-2411-330: It can be observed that the Inversion strategy consistently improves reconstruction performance across all acceleration rates, with the advantage becoming increasingly evident at higher accelerations.
elements.sent-2411-331: This is because subtle structural discrepancies always exist between multimodal images due to inherent differences in imaging physics.
elements.sent-2411-332: At high acceleration rates, the reduced amount of k-space data provides weaker constraints on reconstruction, making the results more vulnerable to these discrepancies.
elements.sent-2411-333: The Inversion strategy can correct these discrepancies and further improve reconstruction accuracy.

elements.subsection-2411-018: Comparison with Previous Reconstruction Methods Using Cross-Modality Priors
elements.sent-2411-345: For reconstruction methods that leverage cross-modality priors, traditional approaches typically construct explicit sparsity or low-rank constraints across multiple contrasts\cite{yang2016sparse,bilgic2011multi,haldar2013improved .}
elements.sent-2411-346: For example, joint-sparsity methods enforce a shared sparse representation in a common transform domain, e.g., group or concatenated wavelets, with coupled $\ell_1$ penalties\cite{bilgic2011multi , while joint low-rank methods stack multi-contrast images or patches into a Casorati matrix and impose low-rank constraints to capture shared anatomical structures\cite{haldar2013improved .}}
elements.sent-2411-347: However, these methods rely on handcrafted priors, which are not trivial and require high computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-348: Previous deep learning approaches often incorporate cross-contrast priors through learned feature-level fusion\cite{polak2020joint,yang2020model, 10645705 .}
elements.sent-2411-349: For example, JVN employs a shared variational reconstruction model that exchanges anatomical information across contrast-specific branches\cite{polak2020joint .}
elements.sent-2411-350: Model-driven attention networks, e.g., MD-DAN and related dual-domain/attention models, explicitly learn the guidance contrast features using attention mechanisms\cite{yang2020model or dual-domain fusion modules\cite{10645705 .}}
elements.sent-2411-351: Although learned fusion methods can capture richer, data-driven relationships, they mainly model coarse structural information, and their performance relies heavily on the network architecture and fusion strategy.
elements.sent-2411-352: In contrast, the proposed SB-based framework offers a pixel-level guidance mechanism that explicitly models the probabilistic translation between the guidance and target modalities.
elements.sent-2411-353: Unlike feature fusion networks that implicitly share information at the feature level, our approach enables direct pixel-wise correspondence and probabilistic coupling between contrasts, leading to more precise structural alignment and preservation of modality-specific details.
elements.sent-2411-354: This pixel-level guidance allows the model to leverage complementary information more effectively while avoiding the over-smoothing issues commonly observed in prior fusion-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-355: There are several limitations in this study.
elements.sent-2411-356: First, the current implementation focuses on reconstructing a single undersampled target contrast (e.g., T2-weighted) guided by a fully sampled reference contrast (e.g., T1-weighted).
elements.sent-2411-357: Extending this framework to simultaneously reconstruct multiple undersampled contrasts with the guidance image is a natural direction for future work.
elements.sent-2411-358: To achieve this, we plan to design multiple parallel Schrödinger Bridge branches to enable collaborative reconstruction across multiple contrasts.
elements.sent-2411-359: Furthermore, inter-contrast correlations among the reconstructed images can be exploited to further improve reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-360: Second, the inference process of $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Inversion involves two stages—initial generation followed by inversion-based refinement—which leads to higher computational cost compared with unrolling-based or other deep learning–based reconstruction methods.}
elements.sent-2411-361: Future work will accelerate the sampling process of Schrödinger bridge-based methods to improve their efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-362: Recent advances, such as DPM-Solver++\cite{ DPM-Solver++ , UniP\cite{ UniPC , Progressive Distillation\cite{ Progressive , and Flow Matching\cite{ Flow_Matching , demonstrate that high-fidelity generation can be achieved with only a few or even a single sampling step by optimizing trajectories or using lightweight solvers.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-363: Incorporating these fast sampling strategies is expected to improve the practicality of the proposed Schrödinger bridge–based reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-364: This study presents $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Inversion, a Schrödinger Bridge-based framework for guided MRI reconstruction that enables pixel-level cross-modality mapping between different image contrasts.}
elements.sent-2411-365: An Inversion strategy is incorporated along the SB path to search for approximately aligned guidance variables based on the initial reconstruction, mitigating the effects of spatial misalignment.
elements.sent-2411-366: Experimental results on paired T1- and T2-weighted datasets demonstrate that $\mathrm{I ^2$SB-Inversion improves reconstruction quality both qualitatively and quantitatively, achieving better artifact suppression and detail preservation compared with SOTA methods.}
